meta.title: BBTI · The Hoops-Fan Personality Test
meta.description: A basketball personality test for people who watch too hard, post too fast, and take losses way too personally. 17 questions, 27 fan types, one card for the group chat.
meta.keywords: basketball personality test,nba fan quiz,hoops fan archetypes,bbti,basketball mbti

landing.subtitle: BBTI is the hoops-fan personality test built to catch how you watch, argue, cope, spend, and spiral.
landing.metaTemplate: {total} questions · 27 fan types · 5 core fan axes
landing.seoSectionAriaLabel: About the BBTI hoops-fan personality test
landing.seoHeading: A personality test for people who take basketball a little too seriously
landing.seoBody: BBTI does not care whether you can hoop. It cares how you react to losses, what you say in the group chat, and whether every big moment becomes content, cope, or combat.
seoPoints: Share card built for the group chat
landing.whyHeading: It reads your fan brain, not your jump shot
landing.whyBody: The way you panic, cope, rank players, defend your team, and post through games says way more about your basketball personality than your actual skill set ever will.
landing.whyAudience: Best for NBA fans, playoff-night sickos, group-chat instigators, hopeless loyalists, and anyone who has ever said "it's not that serious" right after acting like it absolutely was.
landing.errorBody: Open it through a local static server, not by double-clicking the file.

hooks.lead: You blame the refs after every loss,
hooks.punch: but do you actually know ball or just know anger?

hooks.lead: You call people bandwagons all season,
hooks.punch: so why is the newest contender jersey in your cart?

hooks.lead: One transaction sends you into a four-hour spiral,
hooks.punch: and you still think you are a normal fan?

hooks.lead: You say regular season does not matter after losses,
hooks.punch: then quote seeding math after wins.

hooks.lead: You hear one hot take and start reworking top tens,
hooks.punch: so were you here to talk or to hold court?

hooks.lead: You screenshot the dagger before it drops,
hooks.punch: because watching the game is only half the event.

hooks.lead: You keep saying the future is bright,
hooks.punch: like your team is paying hope in installments.

hooks.lead: Every losing streak turns you into a fake GM,
hooks.punch: but could you manage a roster for one week?

hooks.lead: You swear you love the game, not the drama,
hooks.punch: and somehow your favorite moments are still arguments.

hooks.lead: You recycle old basketball trauma like holiday content,
hooks.punch: so how normal was this fandom ever going to be?

hooks.lead: You say "two more minutes" every night,
hooks.punch: and that sentence has never once been true.

hooks.lead: You keep saying you're chill about basketball,
hooks.punch: which would be way more convincing if the chat logs did not exist.

loading.title: Figuring out what kind of fan you really are…
steps: Estimating how much you really do…
Checking your need for an audience…

quiz.progressCopyTemplate: If you make it through all {total}, nobody gets to call you a casual.

result.defaultPrototype: Today, you are your own comp.
result.defaultTriggerNote: Matched through the five-axis profile plus signature answers, rival splits, and conflict weights.
result.noteHiddenQ17: You hit the special BEER ending on Q17 and cleared the required Heat threshold.
result.noteFallbackEmpty: Nothing matched cleanly, so the fallback bench seat was yours.
result.noteFallbackDistance: The top two results were too weak or too close, so the fallback type took over instead of forcing a fake match.
result.evidenceHeading: The two clearest pieces of evidence
result.evidenceNoneHint: This one came from your overall fan profile more than any single answer.
result.rivalHeadingTemplate: You were close to getting tagged as {code}
result.rivalDefaultCopy: The overall profile was close, but a few key answers pulled you back to your actual type.
result.roastEyebrow: Using your own answers against you
result.defaultRoastSummary: You gave the system plenty to work with. It just has not shaped it into a clean roast yet.
result.dimensionsHeading: How your fan profile is built
result.matchesHeading: The three types closest to you
result.shareHeading: Drop the card in the group chat and let the argument begin
result.shareBody: The fun part is not getting the result. The fun part is watching your friends decide whether it is dead-on, offensive, or both.
shareBadges: One tap to generate the card
result.shareImageSubcopy: Post it and see who gets mad first

gallery.title: All 27 hoops-fan archetypes
gallery.body: From GOAT debaters and ref truthers to the hidden beer ending and the fallback bench seat, every fan type is here.

share.tagline: You thought you were watching basketball. Basketball was profiling you.
share.canvasInitError: Result card generation failed: canvas init error

templates: BBTI tagged me as [{code} · {name}]. {quote} See what kind of hoops fan you are: {url}
Apparently my basketball personality is [{code}] {name}. This feels rude and accurate. {url}

personalize.summaryTemplate: You landed on {archetypeName} because you lean hard into {topLabel} and {secondLabel}, while {bottomLabel} barely registers. That mix pushed your fandom all the way into this lane.

modelHighReads.B: You do not just watch games. You audit them.
modelHighReads.L: Loyalty matters to you way more than it does to normal people.
modelHighReads.F: You run hot. Basketball is one of your preferred ways to be mad.
modelHighReads.A: You put real time, money, or effort into this.
modelHighReads.S: You like an audience. A game lands harder when other people know you are watching.

modelLowReads.B: You care more about the overall feeling than the tiny details.
modelLowReads.L: You still have some healthy distance from the laundry.
modelLowReads.F: You do not light on fire that easily, which is rare in fan spaces.
modelLowReads.A: You care, but not enough to hand basketball your whole weekend or wallet.
modelLowReads.S: You do not need to broadcast every reaction to enjoy the game.

Q1.A: One dunk clip turned into a full evening. You were not watching basketball anymore. Basketball was watching you.
Q1.B: "Two more minutes" is one of your most committed long-term lies.
Q1.C: The other side stopped replying ages ago and you were still polishing the seventh draft. That is not discussion. That is cardio.

Q2.A: KD to Golden State barely moved your pulse. Either you are healthy or your fandom is suspiciously shallow.
Q2.B: You tried to be reasonable about it, but that move is still lodged in your chest somewhere.
Q2.C: That one transaction had you posting like it was a national emergency.

Q3.A: You handled the missed call like an adult. Extremely unsettling behavior for a sports fan.
Q3.B: Six smileys and an ellipsis is how basketball fans say, "I am now a threat to the peace."
Q3.C: You did not just get mad. You started assembling evidence.

Q4.A: You heard the take and thought, sure, fair enough. The internet has not fully rotted your brain yet.
Q4.B: The instant "According to who?" appears in your head, it means you were born for all-time debates.
Q4.C: You did not join the conversation. You took custody of it.

Q5.A: Calling 50 a normal beatdown means you still react like a functioning human being to scores.
Q5.B: Anything under 58 barely counts for you. Very specific illness.
Q5.C: The moment you cited "Nuggets 121, Hornets 63 on April 27, 2009" from memory, everybody knew exactly what kind of freak they were dealing with.

Q6.A: Your relationship to the arena is mostly theoretical.
Q6.B: If you do not post proof of attendance, were you even really there?
Q6.C: You do not attend games. You produce them.

Q7.A: A 2-17 start sent you straight to better teams. Honest move. Ruthless, but honest.
Q7.B: You cope by learning the names of teenagers who may or may not save the franchise in three years.
Q7.C: You found a way to turn losing into a personality system. Dark work. Impressive work.

Q8.A: Not recognizing the J.R. clip is like not recognizing a fire alarm. Concerning.
Q8.B: You know exactly what the clip is and still want no part of reliving it.
Q8.C: At this point you repost that clip the way normal people celebrate holidays.

Q9.A: You are not watching the loss. You are backseat coaching in real time.
Q9.B: You open a losing streak with on/off data. That is a very specific kind of sickness.
Q9.C: Three losses in a row and you are already firing people by department.
Q9.D: Blaming the refs is your emotional safe house.
Q9.E: Going to sleep is either wisdom or total system shutdown. Could be either.

Q10.A: Saying "because I love them" about a terrible team means your pain tolerance is historically elite.
Q10.B: Most people hate losing. You keep trying to turn it into a down payment.
Q10.C: You spent all year mocking bandwagons and still ended up admiring whichever team looked prettiest.
Q10.D: At least the Finals-only thing is honest. Fraudulent, but honest.
Q10.E: Your neutrality is so complete it starts to feel suspiciously healthy.

Q11.A: You skip straight to legacy rankings like you are reading a sentence from the league constitution.
Q11.B: The second TS% and BPM enter the chat, the conversation becomes a paper defense.
Q11.C: Pretending to distrust data is often how the smartest data haters end up sounding the most informed.
Q11.D: You do not just want to be right. You want timestamped proof that you got there early.
Q11.E: You understand that posture wins half of internet arguments before content even arrives.

Q12.A: A poster is not just a highlight to you. It is a scientific object for repeat viewing.
Q12.B: You love a shot that feels illegal even before it drops.
Q12.C: Down big and still believing means hope has fully taken over your operating system.
Q12.D: For you, the moment is not complete until the timeline explodes too.
Q12.E: Being able to process a game quietly and alone makes you a very rare species online.

Q13.A: You worry about players like you have been asked to sign their permission slips.
Q13.B: Injury news, and somehow you still found a way to make it about the GM.
Q13.C: You turned a twisted ankle into a labor-law shrug. Cold.
Q13.D: You are not reacting to the person. You are reacting to the future sample.
Q13.E: Nothing says owner brain like immediately cashing out somebody else's ankle for title odds.

Q14.A: You do not just love basketball. You invoice yourself for it.
Q14.B: If nobody knows you watched, a part of you feels like it barely happened.
Q14.C: You keep proving your love with your legs, even when the jumper will not return the favor.
Q14.D: Watching alone is not loneliness for you. It is premium processing time.
Q14.E: You drift through basketball like a person changing radio stations.

Q15.A: You speak with unbelievable confidence for someone whose predictions get publicly executed this often.
Q15.B: You are one pivot table away from applying for a front-office internship.
Q15.C: Nothing hits for you like being right early and having the screenshot ready.
Q15.D: Refusing to predict is maybe the last normal instinct left in sports fandom.

Q16.A: If you have nine circles on one screenshot, you are not posting. You are presenting.
Q16.B: Some people vent after losses. You open Excel.
Q16.C: Three sentences in all caps is not a post. It is an emergency order.
Q16.D: Your concern for officiating paperwork is almost devotional.
Q16.E: Your sarcasm is so sustained it starts to count as a diet.
Q16.F: You do not care whether the stat line is big. You care whether the points were spiritually fraudulent.

Q17.A: Treating an elimination game like a normal day is either zen mastery or evidence the bond was never that deep.
Q17.B: Your hands say nervous. Your mouth says totally chill. Classic fan lie.
Q17.C: At that point you are not getting ready to watch a game. You are staging trial proceedings.
Q17.D: The beer was open before tip. The spiral was always in the mail.
